title: The Rebound Effect

content.statement: Make a resource cheaper to use and you'll often use far more of it, not less.
content.explainer: Efficiency lowers the cost per task, but cheaper tasks invite more tasks, so total consumption can rise even as each use gets leaner. It's why faster tools rarely give you free time and cheaper energy rarely shrinks total demand.
questions: Where has a tool that saved you time per task simply led you to do far more of that task?
When you make something cheaper or faster for customers, does total consumption fall, or explode?
Which 'efficiency win' actually expanded the resource, headcount, or spend it was supposed to shrink?
sop: Identify an efficiency you just gained, a tool, automation, or process that cut cost or time per task.
Estimate how many tasks you did before and how much resource each one consumed.
Estimate how much the lower cost will expand the number of tasks you take on.
Divide total tasks times use-per-task by the new efficiency to project real total consumption (see calculation, page 6).
Set an explicit cap or intention for the freed capacity, so efficiency funds your priorities instead of just more volume.

definitions.def: Getting the same output from less input, less time, energy, or resource per unit of work.
Demand that grows sharply when cost or price falls. When demand is elastic, making something cheaper often means much more of it gets used overall.

content.equation: Total Use = (Tasks × Use per Task) ÷ Efficiency
content.equation_example: Before: (100 tasks × 5 units) ÷ 1 = 500 units used. Efficiency doubles and the lower cost pushes demand to 500 tasks: (500 × 5) ÷ 2 = 1,250 units. Total use rose from 500 to 1,250, high efficiency with elastic demand raises total use; only weak demand would let it fall.
content.closing_statement: Efficiency only frees you if you decide, on purpose, what to do with the room it opens up.
content.question: Where has a tool that saved you time per task simply led you to do far more of that task?

caption:
Principle Nº 338: The Rebound Effect

Make a resource cheaper to use and you'll often use far more of it, not less.

Ask yourself: Where has a tool that saved you time per task simply led you to do far more of that task?
